title: Quick check: Events to listen to

questions.prompt: For a subscription SaaS, what's the minimal complete set of webhook events?

choices.text: ~13 events covering the lifecycle: checkout.session.completed, payment_intent.succeeded/failed, customer.subscription.created/updated/deleted, invoice.paid/payment_failed/payment_action_required, customer.subscription.trial_will_end, charge.refunded, charge.dispute.created. Subscribing to specific event types beats 'all events' — less noise, easier handler, lower cost.

questions.explanation: Subscription lifecycle needs creation, updates (plan changes, trials, status transitions), invoices (paid/failed/auth-required), trial-end nudges, refunds, disputes. ~13 specific event types covers it; 'all events' is noise.

questions.prompt: Why pair webhook handlers with a daily reconciliation job?

choices.text: Webhooks can fail or be missed (handler bugs, downtime, edge cases). A daily reconciliation job compares local DB state to Stripe's current state and syncs discrepancies. Belt-and-suspenders: webhooks for immediate consistency; reconciliation for catching drift. Saves you from customer-support tickets when bugs slip through.

questions.explanation: Webhooks + reconciliation = robust state sync. Webhooks are fast but fallible; reconciliation is slow but exhaustive. Together they catch issues neither alone would.
